title: GPU
description: Parallel hardware accelerators with many execution units suited to dense matrix and vector work common in neural network inference and training.
openingSummary: A graphics processing unit (GPU) is hardware built around thousands of small parallel execution units that excel at repeating the same math across large blocks of numbers. Neural networks spend much of their time on dense matrix and vector multiplication, which is why GPUs have become the default accelerator for model workloads. What matters in practice is not just how many cores exist, but how much video random access memory (VRAM) the device can hold and how quickly it can move weights, activations, and key-value cache blocks through that memory.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A graphics processing unit (GPU) is parallel hardware designed to run the same operation across many data points at once. Modern GPUs contain thousands of lightweight cores grouped into streaming multiprocessors. Neural network layers are mostly dense matrix multiplications and similar vector work, so this parallelism maps naturally to the repetitive math models need.

whereItSits.title: Where It Sits
whereItSits.body: A GPU sits beneath the inference engine and serving systems that schedule requests. The learned model weights define what computation should happen. The GPU supplies the parallel arithmetic and local high-bandwidth memory that make that computation fast enough to serve at scale. CPUs still matter for orchestration, networking, and small irregular work, but the heavy matrix math usually lands on the GPU.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: During a forward pass, the runtime loads weights into video random access memory (VRAM) and dispatches kernels that multiply large matrices and vectors across many cores at once. Memory bandwidth sets how quickly weights and active state can move between VRAM and the compute units, while VRAM capacity sets how much model state can stay resident at once, including weights, activations, and growing key-value cache entries. Many GPUs also include tensor cores, specialized blocks that execute matrix multiply-accumulate operations in fewer cycles than general cores. Tensor cores do not change the mathematics of the model; they accelerate the same dense linear algebra under supported data types and shapes.

practicalImpact.title: Practical Impact
practicalImpact.body: GPU hardware shapes what models can run and how quickly data moves, even before serving policy enters the picture. A model may fit in VRAM on paper and still run slowly when memory bandwidth cannot feed the cores fast enough. Larger models, longer contexts, and more concurrent sessions all compete for the same VRAM and bandwidth budget. Operators often respond with quantization, cache policies, or tiered placement, but those are runtime choices made because the underlying GPU has finite parallel capacity and finite local memory.

related.title: Related To
related.body: The related pages below connect GPU hardware limits to nearby serving systems and glossary entries for memory bandwidth, quantization, roofline reasoning, and runtime placement.

systemFlow.alt: Model weights and live state load into VRAM, thousands of parallel cores run dense matrix and vector math, memory bandwidth moves data between VRAM and compute units, and tensor cores accelerate supported matrix blocks.
systemFlow.caption: Parallel cores do the math, VRAM holds resident state, and memory bandwidth decides how quickly that state reaches the cores.
